nativeRange.habitat: Sandy substrate near rocky shorelines and transition zones between rock and open sand; found over areas of fine sand where males hover and sift for invertebrates
nativeRange.biotope: Lake Malawi — sandy benthic zone near rock-sand interfaces

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (5+ cm) for natural sifting behaviour
open sandy areas for foraging and male territory display
rock piles or caves along back and sides for territory boundaries and retreats
minimal planting — species inhabits unvegetated sandy zones; hardy Vallisneria along edges acceptable
dim-to-moderate lighting to reflect rocky-shore biotope

diet.notes: Insectivore in the wild; sifts mouthfuls of sand to extract invertebrates (chironomid larvae, crustaceans, worms). In aquaria, accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and cyclops. Spirulina-based flake can supplement mineral nutrition. Avoid excessive beef heart or high-protein mammalian matter — associated with Malawi bloat in Haplochromine cichlids. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily; sand-sifting behaviour provides enrichment and should not be prevented.

goodWith: other Aulonocara species (peacock cichlids) — mix carefully, one male per species
Copadichromis and other non-aggressive Haplochromine cichlids
larger peaceful Synodontis catfish (S. petricola, S. multipunctatus)
Lethrinops and Protomelas species of similar size
avoidWith: mbuna (Pseudotropheus, Melanochromis, Labeotropheus) — significantly more aggressive and will stress or injure peacocks
multiple males of the same Aulonocara species without very large tanks — dominant male harassment
small fish or fry that will be eaten
other peacock hybrid strains if maintaining pure-line specimens

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo gallery for Aulonocara baenschi. Reviewer should verify individual image license before upload.
Wikimedia Commons category page for Aulonocara baenschi. Multiple images likely available under CC licenses; reviewer to select best-quality male in breeding coloration.

summary: The Baenschi Peacock (Sunshine Peacock) is a vibrant Lake Malawi Haplochromine prized for the brilliant yellow-gold coloration of dominant males. A sand-sifting insectivore, it is less aggressive than mbuna cichlids and can be community-kept with other peacocks in a large alkaline tank with open sandy zones.
careNotes: Aulonocara baenschi is endemic to Lake Malawi, where it inhabits the sandy transition zones adjacent to rocky shorelines — primarily at Nkhomo Reef near Benga (from which the trade name 'Nkhomo Benga Peacock' derives). Males in peak condition display striking yellow-gold body coloration with blue facial iridescence; females and subdominant males are drab olive-brown. Dominant males are territorial toward conspecific males and competing peacock species but are substantially less aggressive than mbuna. A typical harem arrangement — one dominant male with 3–5 females — works well in a 200–300 L tank with 120+ cm footprint. Maintain hard, highly alkaline water (pH 7.8–8.6, gH 10–20) replicating Lake Malawi chemistry; soft or acidic water causes stress, colour loss, and increased disease susceptibility. A 5+ cm sand bed is important for natural sifting enrichment; preventing sand access leads to boredom and feeding frustration. Rockwork should define territorial boundaries and provide retreats for submissive fish, but large open sandy areas must remain. Malawi bloat (internal bacterial or protozoal infection) is a significant risk if diet is inappropriate (avoid high-fat mammalian proteins) or water quality lapses. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are strongly advised. IMPORTANT: Aulonocara baenschi is frequently hybridized in the trade with A. stuartgranti (Regal/Flavescent Peacock) and other Aulonocara species — hybrid males often display similar bright yellow coloration and are sold under the same trade names. Aquarists seeking to maintain pure-line specimens should source only from reputable specialist Malawi breeders with documented lineage.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning is initiated by male courtship display over open sand. The female lays eggs on the substrate and immediately picks them up into her buccal cavity. Incubation lasts approximately 21–28 days at 26°C, during which the female holds 15–50 fry and does not feed. Females should be isolated in a breeding tank or a quiet section of the display tank prior to spitting fry to prevent exhaustion from harassment. Fry are released as free-swimming juveniles capable of accepting finely crushed dry food and baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii) from day one. Females may reabsorb eggs or spit prematurely if stressed; a minimum 6-week rest period between spawning cycles is recommended. Hybridization with other Aulonocara species occurs readily in mixed-species tanks — keep only one male peacock species per tank to prevent unwanted crosses.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aulonocara baenschi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy, native range (Nkhomo Reef near Benga, Lake Malawi), adult size (males to 13 cm), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.5, temperature 24–28°C, hardness 143–357 ppm), diet (insectivore — sand-sifting for chironomid larvae and invertebrates), compatibility (non-mbuna, harem keeping), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day incubation), and trade hybridization caution.

additional.name: FishBase — Aulonocara baenschi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (max ~13 cm TL), native range (endemic to Lake Malawi), depth range, and diet classification (invertivore). Distribution confirmed as Nkhomo Reef / Benga area. IUCN status Least Concern noted.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Aquarium Co-Op general African cichlid/peacock guide. Confirmed pH 7.5–8.5, temperature 76–82°F (24–28°C), tank size recommendation of 55+ gallons (approx. 208 L) for peacock species, hard alkaline water requirement, sand substrate preference, and carnivore/insectivore diet. Notes on mbuna incompatibility and Malawi bloat risk.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Aulonocara species guide
additional.notes: Confirmed Aulonocara baenschi as one of the most popular peacock species in the trade; discussed hybridization problem with A. stuartgranti and importance of sourcing from specialist breeders for pure lines. Noted 'Nkhomo Benga' and 'Sunshine Peacock' trade names. Breeding and harem structure details aligned with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Peacock Cichlids of Lake Malawi
additional.notes: Background on Lake Malawi Haplochromine ecology, Aulonocara sand-sifting foraging strategy, and lateral line system used to detect invertebrate movements below sand surface. Confirmed that peacocks occupy sand-rock interface zones distinct from mbuna rockwork habitat. Hybridization in the trade discussed as a significant issue for species integrity.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; 21–28 day oral incubation of 15–50 fry at 26°C. Isolate brooding females to prevent stress-induced premature spitting. Fry accept micro-crushed food and baby brine shrimp from day one. Hybridizes readily with other Aulonocara species — single-species male setup strongly recommended for pure-line breeding.
